parent_section: (SDTMIG v3.4)
verbatim: 7 Trial Design Model Datasets

parent_section: §7 [TRIAL DESIGN MODEL DATASETS]
verbatim: 7.1 Introduction to Trial Design Model Datasets

parent_section: §7.1 Introduction to Trial Design Model Datasets
verbatim: 7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: ICH E3, Guidance for Industry, Structure and Content of Clinical Study Reports (available at http://www.ich.ch/products/guidelines/), Section 9.1, calls for a brief, clear description of the overall plan and design of the study, and supplies examples of charts and diagrams for this purpose in Annex IIIa and Annex IIIb.

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: Each Annex corresponds to an example trial, and each shows a diagram describing the study design and a table showing the schedule of assessments.

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: The Trial Design Model provides a standardized way to describe those aspects of the planned conduct of a clinical trial shown in the study design diagrams of these examples.

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: The standard Trial Design Datasets will allow reviewers to:

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: Clearly and quickly grasp the design of a clinical trial

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: Compare the designs of different trials

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: Search a data warehouse for clinical trials with certain features

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: Compare planned and actual treatments and visits for subjects in a clinical trial

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: Modeling a clinical trial in this standardized way requires the explicit statement of certain decision rules that may not be addressed or may be vague or ambiguous in the usual prose protocol document.

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: Prospective modeling of the design of a clinical trial should lead to a clearer, better protocol.

parent_section: §7.1.1 Purpose of the Trial Design Model
verbatim: Retrospective modeling of the design of a clinical trial should ensure a clear description of how the trial protocol was interpreted by the sponsor.

parent_section: §7.1 Introduction to Trial Design Model Datasets
verbatim: 7.1.2 Definitions of Trial Design Concepts

parent_section: §7.1.2 Definitions of Trial Design Concepts
verbatim: A clinical trial is a scientific experiment involving human subjects, intended to address certain scientific questions (i.e., the objectives of the trial).

parent_section: §7.1.2 Definitions of Trial Design Concepts
verbatim: See the CDISC Glossary (https://www.cdisc.org/standards/glossary) for more complete definitions of trial and objective.

parent_section: §7.1.2 Definitions of Trial Design Concepts
verbatim: | Concept | Definition |

parent_section: §7.1.2 Definitions of Trial Design Concepts
verbatim: | Trial design | The design of a clinical trial is a plan for what will be done to subjects and what data will be collected about them, in the course of the trial, to address the trial's objectives. |

parent_section: §7.1.2 Definitions of Trial Design Concepts
verbatim: | Epoch | As part of the design of a trial, the planned period of subjects' participation in the trial is divided into epochs. Each epoch is a period of time that serves a purpose in the trial as a whole. That purpose will be at the level of the primary objectives of the trial. Typically, the purpose of an epoch will be to expose subjects to a treatment or to prepare for such a treatment period (e.g., determine subject eligibility, washout previous treatments), or to gather data on subjects after a treatment has ended. Note that at this high level, a "treatment" is a treatment strategy, which may be simple (e.g., exposure to a single drug at a single dose) or complex. Complex treatment strategies could involve tapering through several doses, titrating dose according to clinical criteria, complex regimens involving multiple drugs, or strategies for adding or dropping drugs according to clinical criteria. |

parent_section: §7.1.2 Definitions of Trial Design Concepts
verbatim: | Arm | An arm is a planned path through the trial. This path covers the entire time of the trial. The group of subjects assigned to a planned path is also often colloquially called an "arm." The group of subjects assigned to an arm is also often called a "treatment group"; in this sense, an arm is equivalent to a treatment group. |

parent_section: §7.1.2 Definitions of Trial Design Concepts
verbatim: | Study cell | Each planned path through the trial (i.e., each arm) is divided into pieces, 1 for each epoch. Each of these pieces is called a study cell. Thus, there is a study cell for each combination of arm and epoch. Each study cell represents an implementation of the purpose of its associated epoch. For an epoch whose purpose is to expose subjects to treatment, each study cell associated with the treatment epoch, 1 for each arm. Each of these study cells exposes the subject to 1 of the 3 study treatments. Another example involving more complex treatment strategies would be a trial comparing the effects of cycles of chemotherapy drug A given alone or in combination with drug B, where drug B is given as a pretreatment to each cycle of drug A. |
